topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara stared at the screen, her breath catching in her throat. The headline, the opening paragraph—it was all hers. The meticulous research, the late nights wrestling with the narrative, the sheer force of her own voice, all now belonging to a stranger. A cold knot tightened in her stomach, spreading outward until her hands began to tremble slightly on the keyboard. She reread the publication details, the byline a stark, unfamiliar name. She leaned back in her chair, the cheap plastic groaning under her weight, and slowly pushed her glasses up the bridge of her nose. "This can't be right," she whispered, the sound thin and reedy in the quiet office. She started clicking through the archives, a frantic, almost desperate rhythm to her mouse movements, searching for any anomaly, any clue that this was a mistake, a clerical error. The silence of the room amplified the frantic drumming inside her chest.

[story 2]

Mark felt the blood drain from his face. He picked up the printed copy of the magazine, his fingers slick with sudden sweat. The article detailed his groundbreaking analysis of urban decay, the nuanced perspective he had painstakingly crafted over six months. Yet, the name emblazoned beneath the title belonged to a ghost—a name he didn't recognize, a name that felt utterly alien on his tongue. He dropped the paper onto his desk with a sharp slap that echoed too loudly. He stood up abruptly, pacing the small confines of his apartment, running a hand through his already disheveled hair. His jaw was tight, his breathing shallow. He kept replaying the submission process in his head, trying to reconstruct the sequence of events, but the memory felt slippery, distorted. "Who signed off on this?" he muttered to the empty room, his voice laced with a sharp, brittle edge.

[story 3]

When the notification finally appeared, Liam didn't move for a full minute. He simply sat there, the glow of the monitor reflecting in his wide, unblinking eyes. The article, his magnum opus on renewable energy policy, was live. But the author listed was someone else entirely. A sudden wave of nausea washed over him, making the room tilt slightly. He slowly reached out, his fingers hovering over the mouse, hesitating before clicking on the author's profile. The unfamiliar face stared back—a professional, polished, utterly disconnected from the passion that fueled the writing. He let out a long, slow exhale, the tension in his shoulders easing just a fraction, replaced by a profound sense of disorientation. He needed a moment to just breathe, to let the sheer absurdity of the situation sink in.
